section.title: The Reign of the Righteous King
section.sectionRationale: The great royal psalm for Solomon, closing Book II with the note that 'the prayers of David son of Jesse are ended,' its vision of a universal, everlasting, poor-lifting reign so far exceeds any human king that it has always been read as reaching beyond Solomon to the Messiah, the true King.
section.thesis: Prayed over Solomon, this psalm's vision of a king who judges with righteousness, rescues the needy, and reigns from sea to sea until all nations are blessed in him finds its only adequate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the true and everlasting King.
section.frameNote: Six movements: a prayer for a just king, verses 1 to 4; a reign marked by peace, verses 5 to 7; universal dominion, verses 8 to 11; compassion for the needy, verses 12 to 14; enduring blessing and fame, verses 15 to 17; closing doxology and the end of David's prayers, verses 18 to 20.

units.label: A prayer for a just king

verses.text: Of Solomon. Endow the king with Your {{mishpat|justice}}, O God, and the son of the king with Your righteousness.
May he judge Your people with righteousness and Your afflicted with justice.
May the mountains bring peace to the people, and the hills bring righteousness.
May he vindicate the afflicted among the people; may he save the children of the needy and crush the oppressor.

units.label: A reign marked by peace
units.note: as long as the sun shines

verses.text: May they fear him as long as the sun shines, as long as the moon remains, through all generations.
May he be like rain that falls on freshly cut grass, like spring showers that water the earth.
May the righteous flourish in his days and prosperity abound until the moon is no more.
May he rule from sea to sea, and from the Euphrates to the ends of the earth.
May the nomads bow before him, and his enemies lick the dust.
May the kings of Tarshish and distant shores bring tribute; may the kings of Sheba and Seba offer gifts.
May all kings bow down to him and all nations serve him.
For he will deliver the needy who cry out and the afflicted who have no helper.
He will take pity on the poor and needy and save the lives of the oppressed.
He will redeem them from oppression and violence, for their blood is precious in his sight.
Long may he live! May gold from Sheba be given him. May people ever pray for him; may they bless him all day long.
May there be an abundance of grain in the land; may it sway atop the hills. May its fruit trees flourish like the forests of Lebanon, the people of its cities like the grass of the field.
May his name endure forever; may his name continue as long as the sun shines. In him may all nations be {{barak|blessed}}; may they call him blessed.

units.label: Doxology and the end of David's prayers

verses.text: Blessed be the LORD God, the God of Israel, who alone does marvelous deeds.
And blessed be His glorious name forever; may all the earth be filled with His glory. Amen and amen.
Thus conclude the prayers of David son of Jesse.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the exercise of fair rule. The psalm opens by asking God to give the king His own justice, not merely human fairness, so that the king's rule reflects God's own character.

barak.gloss: To bless, to kneel in homage. Verse 17's promise that 'all nations be blessed' in this king echoes the promise to Abraham, Genesis 12:3, tying the royal hope of this psalm to God's ancient covenant purpose for the whole world.

basic.context: Prayed 'of Solomon,' likely David's prayer for his son at the transfer of the crown, this psalm asks God to give the king His own justice and righteousness so that his reign brings peace, rescues the poor, and extends to the ends of the earth.
basic.hermeneutics: No earthly king, not even Solomon at his height, ever ruled with the perfection, universality, and endless duration this psalm describes. Ancient and Christian readers alike have taken this as evidence the psalm intentionally reaches past Solomon to the Messiah.
basic.originalLanguages: The king is asked to embody mishpat, justice, God's own standard of right rule, and the psalm's climax promises that in him all nations will be barak, blessed, deliberately echoing God's ancient promise to Abraham that all peoples would be blessed through his offspring.
basic.typology: The universal, everlasting, poor-defending king of this psalm is a type fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the true Son of David whose kingdom has no end, whose care for the needy exceeds any earthly ruler's, and in whom the Abrahamic blessing to all nations finally comes to pass.
basic.christ: Every feature of this ideal king, universal reign from sea to sea, everlasting name, compassion for the poor, blessing to all nations, is fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of David whose kingdom Luke 1:32-33 declares will never end and before whom Revelation 11:15 pictures every kingdom of the earth finally bowing.
basic.god: God is the source of the king's justice and righteousness; the king's rule is asked for, not assumed, showing that true and lasting authority to rule rightly comes from God alone.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 11:1-10 and Isaiah 9:6-7 describe the same righteous, peace-bringing king. Galatians 3:16 identifies Abraham's promised offspring, through whom all nations are blessed, as Christ. Revelation 11:15 proclaims the kingdoms of this world have become the kingdom of the Lord and of His Christ.
basic.oneStory: This prayer for Solomon's righteous, universal, poor-lifting reign was too large for Solomon or any human king to fulfill, and so it points forward through the whole story of Scripture to Jesus Christ, the true King in whom God's ancient promise that all nations would be blessed finally comes true.

context.literary: A royal psalm structured as a cascade of petitions, 'may he... may he...', moving from justice and peace to universal dominion, compassion for the needy, enduring fame, and closing in doxology, with an inclusio linking the opening plea for justice to the closing praise of God's marvelous deeds.
context.historical: Traditionally read as David's prayer over Solomon near the transfer of the throne, 1 Kings 1-2; Solomon's own reign did see peace, wealth, and visits from foreign rulers such as the Queen of Sheba, 1 Kings 10, though on a scale far smaller than this psalm envisions.
context.canonical: Closing Book II with the note 'the prayers of David son of Jesse are ended,' this psalm functions as a hinge, summing up the hopes placed in the Davidic line and handing them forward, unresolved by any single king, into the rest of the Psalter and the prophets.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To pray that God would grant the newly crowned king His own justice and righteousness, producing a reign of peace, protection for the needy, and blessing that extends to every nation on earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm is a prayer and hope for a specific king, not a description of any king's actual reign as it occurred; it prescribes that all who hold authority should govern with God-given justice and special care for the poor and needy.

debates.question: Is Psalm 72 a prayer for Solomon that was simply never fully realized, or a deliberately messianic psalm from the start?

views.label: A historical prayer for Solomon whose language is simply the exalted style of ancient royal psalms
views.case: The superscription names Solomon, and similar hyperbolic language for kings appears in other ancient Near Eastern royal texts and hymns, so the grand language need not point beyond Solomon himself.

views.label: A prayer intentionally written to exceed any human king, anticipating the Messiah
views.case: Details such as an everlasting name, worldwide dominion, and all nations being blessed in him go far beyond what any historical monarch achieved, and both Jewish and Christian tradition have long read the psalm as messianic.

debates.bearing: Either way, Solomon's real but limited reign of peace and wisdom becomes a signpost whose unfulfilled excess drives the reader to look for a greater King, whom the New Testament identifies as Jesus.

study.originalLanguages: The king is asked to embody mishpat, justice, God's own standard, verse 1, and the psalm's climax promises all nations will be barak, blessed, in him, verse 17, deliberately echoing the promise given to Abraham that all peoples of the earth would be blessed through his offspring.

typology.type: The universal, everlasting king who rules from sea to sea, verses 8, 17
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the Son of David whose kingdom Luke 1:32-33 declares will never end.

typology.type: The king who saves the needy and redeems them from oppression, verses 12-14
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, who rescues the poor and afflicted and whose own blood, precious beyond any other, purchased their redemption, 1 Peter 1:18-19.

typology.type: All nations blessed in him, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the promised offspring of Abraham through whom every nation is blessed, Galatians 3:16.

study.christ: Every line of this psalm's hope outgrows Solomon and finds its fulfillment in Jesus. His kingdom is everlasting, Luke 1:32-33; His compassion for the poor and needy exceeds any earthly ruler's; His precious blood, echoing verse 14, redeemed a people, 1 Peter 1:18-19; and in Him, the offspring of Abraham, all nations are finally and truly blessed, Galatians 3:16, until every kingdom of the earth becomes the kingdom of the Lord and of His Christ, Revelation 11:15.
study.god: God is the source from whom the king's justice and righteousness must come, the one who alone does marvelous deeds, and whose glory the psalm prays will fill the whole earth.

crossReferences.note: Describes the coming king whose government and peace will have no end, echoing this psalm's hope.
Identifies Christ as the promised offspring of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed, fulfilling verse 17.
Proclaims the everlasting, universal reign this psalm prays for, finally realized in Christ's kingdom.

study.oneStory: This prayer for Solomon's righteous, peace-bringing, poor-lifting, universal reign was always too large for any human king to fulfill, and so it hands forward, through the whole story of Scripture, the hope of a true King, fulfilled in Jesus Christ, in whom every nation is at last blessed as God promised Abraham.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God to give the king, verse 1?

options.text: God's own justice and righteousness.
options.note: The king's fitness to rule is asked to come directly from God's character, not human wisdom alone.

options.text: Great wealth and many horses.
options.note: The prayer centers on justice and righteousness, not material accumulation.

options.text: A large standing army.
options.note: The psalm never asks for military strength as the foundation of the reign.

options.text: Freedom from all responsibility.
options.note: The opposite; the king is asked to take up the weighty responsibility of just rule.

quiz.q: How far does the psalm envision this king's rule extending, verse 8?

options.text: From sea to sea and from the Euphrates to the ends of the earth.
options.note: This universal scope exceeds any historical king's actual territory.

options.text: Only within the city of Jerusalem.
options.note: The psalm's vision is explicitly universal, not merely local.

options.text: Only over the twelve tribes of Israel.
options.note: The vision goes beyond Israel to all nations and kings of the earth.

options.text: Only as far as neighboring Egypt.
options.note: The psalm names the Euphrates and 'the ends of the earth,' far exceeding Egypt alone.

quiz.q: What does the king do for the needy and afflicted, verses 12-14?

options.text: He delivers, takes pity on, and redeems them from oppression and violence, valuing their blood as precious.
options.note: This compassionate rescue is central to the psalm's picture of true kingship.

options.text: He taxes them further to fund his court.
options.note: The opposite; he defends and rescues the poor rather than burdening them.

options.text: He ignores them in favor of the wealthy.
options.note: The psalm specifically highlights his care for those who have no helper.

options.text: He exiles them from the land.
options.note: The text describes rescue and redemption, not exile.

quiz.q: What does verse 17 promise will happen to all nations in this king?

options.text: All nations will be blessed in him, echoing God's promise to Abraham.
options.note: This deliberately recalls Genesis 12:3, tying the psalm to God's covenant purpose for the whole world.

options.text: All nations will be destroyed by him.
options.note: The psalm envisions blessing, not destruction, extending to the nations.

options.text: All nations will forget his name.
options.note: The opposite; his name is promised to endure forever, verse 17.

options.text: All nations will be forced into slavery.
options.note: The psalm describes willing blessing and homage, not forced slavery.

quiz.q: What note closes this psalm and Book II of the Psalter, verse 20?

options.text: "Thus conclude the prayers of David son of Jesse."
options.note: This marks a deliberate structural close to the collection of David's prayers at this point in the Psalter.

options.text: A call to build a new temple.
options.note: No such call appears; the psalm closes in doxology and this concluding note.

options.text: A warning against future kings.
options.note: The psalm closes in blessing and praise, not warning.

options.text: A genealogy of Solomon's descendants.
options.note: No genealogy appears; the psalm ends with worship and this closing note.

questions: This psalm prays that the king would receive God's own justice and righteousness, not merely human wisdom, verse 1. Where in your own responsibilities do you need to ask God for His justice rather than trusting your own judgment?
The ideal king rescues the needy who have no helper, verses 12-14. Who around you has no helper, and what would it look like to reflect the compassion of the true King toward them?

This psalm's vision, universal peace, an everlasting name, blessing to all nations, was always too large for Solomon. Where do you see this hope beginning to be fulfilled in Christ's kingdom today, and where are you still waiting for it in full?

Verse 17 ties this king's reign to God's ancient promise that all nations would be blessed through Abraham's offspring. How does knowing you are part of that blessed multitude in Christ shape your sense of belonging to God's people?

The psalm closes by noting that David's prayers are concluded, yet the hope it expresses continues through the rest of Scripture. What unfulfilled hope in your own life do you need to entrust to God's larger, unfolding purposes?

translationNotes.note: Some ancient manuscripts, reflected in a few translations, read 'He shall endure as long as the sun,' rather than 'may they fear him,' a small variant that does not change the psalm's overall hope for an enduring, reverenced reign.
'The Euphrates' renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning 'the River,' the standard way the Euphrates is designated in the Old Testament as the far boundary of the promised land, Genesis 15:18.
'May his name continue' can also be rendered 'may his name increase,' both readings pointing to an ever-growing, never-fading reputation for this king, in contrast to every merely human ruler's fame.
